scenarios.title: Domestic Company — Normal Tax at 25%

schedulesToFill.notes: Compute MAT even if normal tax is higher

stepByStepFilling.action: Enter company details, CIN, industry code, nature of business (IT services)
Net profit per P&L: ₹20L. Add back book depreciation: ₹3L. Deduct IT Act depreciation: ₹4.5L. Adjusted income: ₹18.5L
Book profit: ₹20L. MAT @ 15%: ₹3L. Normal tax @ 25%: ₹4.625L. Normal tax > MAT → normal tax applies.
Tax ₹4,62,500. Surcharge (income < ₹1Cr): nil. Cess 4%: ₹18,500. Total: ₹4,81,000.

redFlagsInScenario: MAT computation mandatory even if not applicable
Book depreciation vs IT Act depreciation must be reconciled
commonMistakesToAvoid: Applying 30% rate (only for turnover > ₹400Cr in prior year)

scenarios.title: Company Where MAT Exceeds Normal Tax

taxpayerProfile.name: DEF Manufacturing Ltd
taxpayerProfile.taxRateApplicable: 25% — but MAT overrides due to large b/f losses

schedulesToFill.notes: Carry-forward losses reduce normal taxable income
MAT > normal tax — MAT is payable
Prior year business losses being set off

stepByStepFilling.action: Net profit as per P&L: ₹80L (book profit). Book depreciation: ₹25L. IT Act depreciation: ₹30L. Normal income before CFL set-off: ₹75L.
Set off brought-forward business losses: ₹70L. Normal taxable income: ₹5L. Normal tax: 25% × ₹5L = ₹1.25L.
MAT on book profit ₹80L: 15% × ₹80L = ₹12L. MAT ₹12L > Normal tax ₹1.25L → PAY MAT.
MAT credit (MATC) generated: ₹12L - ₹1.25L = ₹10.75L → Carry forward for 15 years.
Tax payable: ₹12L + cess 4% ₹48,000 = ₹12,48,000. TDS credits: ₹2L. Advance tax: ₹8L. Self-assessment: ₹2,48,000.

redFlagsInScenario: MAT based on book profit, not taxable income
MATC credit must be tracked — expires after 15 AYs
commonMistakesToAvoid: Paying only normal tax when MAT is higher
Not generating MATC credit entry for future years

scenarios.title: Company Opting Section 115BAA (22% Flat Rate, No MAT)

taxpayerProfile.name: GHI Retail Pvt Ltd
taxpayerProfile.type: Domestic company, exercised 115BAA option in AY 2022-23
taxpayerProfile.regimeChoice: 115BAA — irrevocable, no MAT, no profit-linked deductions

schedulesToFill.notes: 22% flat rate, no MAT computation needed

stepByStepFilling.action: Business income: ₹50L. No 80IC/10AA deductions allowed under 115BAA.
Tax: 22% × ₹50L = ₹11L. Surcharge: 10% if income > ₹10Cr — ₹50L < ₹10Cr → nil. Cess 4%: ₹44,000. Total: ₹11,44,000.
No MAT schedule required under 115BAA.

redFlagsInScenario: Option is irrevocable — cannot switch back to normal rate

scenarios.title: Loss-Making Company — Carry Forward of Losses

schedulesToFill.notes: MAT on book profit — if book loss, MAT = 0

stepByStepFilling.action: Revenue: ₹40L. Expenses (salaries, rent, cloud infra, etc.): ₹65L. Net book loss: ₹25L.
MAT: Book profit is negative (₹25L loss) → MAT = 0. No tax payable.
CFL: Business loss ₹25L. Must be filed on time (Oct 31, 2026 for audit companies) to carry forward.
Unabsorbed depreciation: ₹3L → Carry forward indefinitely (no 8-year limit for depreciation).
Return must still be filed to register the loss for carry-forward purposes.

redFlagsInScenario: Startup losses can be carried forward for 7 years under startup scheme if DPIIT registered
commonMistakesToAvoid: Not filing ITR just because no tax liability — loss carry-forward requires timely filing

scenarios.title: Foreign Company with Indian Royalty Income (DTAA)

taxpayerProfile.name: XYZ Software Corp (USA)
taxpayerProfile.type: Foreign company — no PE in India
taxpayerProfile.taxRateApplicable: 40% domestic OR 15% DTAA (India-USA), whichever is lower

schedulesToFill.notes: Not applicable — foreign company has no foreign income reporting

stepByStepFilling.action: Royalty received from Indian companies for software license: ₹25L
DTAA India-USA Article 12: Royalties taxed at 15% (lower than domestic 40%). Company claims DTAA benefit.
TDS u/s 195 deducted by Indian payer at DTAA 15%: ₹3.75L. Self-assessment for cess: ₹15,000.

redFlagsInScenario: Form 10F must be filed (Tax Residency Certificate from US IRS required for DTAA benefit)
No PE in India must be confirmed — if PE exists, business profits are taxed at 40%
commonMistakesToAvoid: Paying 40% domestic rate when DTAA gives 15%
Not filing Form 10F before claiming DTAA benefit
